system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 1: Module 1 - Chord Melody Quickstart
Pages 15-31 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

GEORGE VAN EPS – KISSES




George Van Eps had a jazz guitar career that spanned 60 years and was still performing into his 80s.

In the 1930s he invented a model of guitar adding an extra bass string to a standard six-string
instrument. This new 7-string guitar enabled him to play low basslines in his arrangements and is a
defining feature of his sound.


Module 1 - Chord
Melody Quickstart


How to Arrange Chord Melodies – The Basic in a Nutshell

Here’s a ‘bird’s eye overview’ on how to arrange a chord melody.

We’ll be going into much more detail later in the course, but in this section, you will learn what you
need to do in a nutshell for creating your own chord melody arrangements:

   Pick a suitable tune
   Change the key if necessary for better range and playability
   Learn the melody on top 2 (or 3) strings
   Choose chord voicings to add below melody notes


Step 1 - Picking a Suitable Tune

Not all tunes are playable as chord melodies or can be difficult.

For your first arrangement, I recommend you stick to ballads, rather than the faster bebop tunes. The
slower tempo and longer-held melody notes in ballads will be much easier for your first project.

I’m going to use Autumn Leaves for this example as it ticks all these boxes – it can be played as a
ballad and it has some long melody notes, a perfect choice.




   Play audio example 1-1


Step 2 - Change Key if Necessary for Range and Playability

On guitar, finding a suitable range for your arrangement is important:

   If the arrangement is too high, it will sound like it doesn’t have enough depth.
   On the other hand, if your arrangement is too low in the register, it will sound too dark and
   muddy.

Finding the ideal range is usually what will dictate your choice of key. You’ll need to experiment to
find a key that works.

A good rule of thumb:


 Search for a key where you can play the melody on the top 3 strings, roughly anywhere from the
 3rd to the 9th fret.


Some of those notes of Autumn Leaves in Gm are too low – notice in the previous example how many
notes are on the 4th and 5th strings.

So instead, let’s try the other key Autumn Leaves is commonly played in – E minor:




   Play audio example 1-2


Step 3 - Learn the Melody on the Top 2 (or 3) Strings

This is where you need to experiment a bit. We need to be able to play the melody on the top 2
strings (maybe dipping into the 3rd string occasionally) and around the 3rd to 9th fret ideally.

Let’s now change the TAB of the melody line so that it mainly sits on the top 2 strings.

This will mean you will need to play more horizontally over the fretboard, rather than staying in a
single position. Watch the video below to notice how the fingering has been adapted with this in mind.

Step 4 - Choose Chord Voicings to Add Below Melody Notes

Now we are set up for success for a chord melody arrangement.

With the melody notes fingered on the high strings, we can now place chord voicings underneath
the melody notes.

Because the melody notes are mainly on the 1st and 2nd strings, we have enough available strings
for the voicings underneath.




  Play audio example 1-3


We’ll get into much more detail later in the course as to how to work out the best chord voicings to
apply to the melody notes.

But for now, an important thing to remember is:




 You don’t need a chord on every melody note.




Too many chords can make your arrangement sound cluttered and make it difficult to play.

Notice on the above example, I mainly place chords on the long-held melody notes. It sounds great
and is easy on the hands.

Also, notice how I adapted the melody in the above example. The long melody notes ring out quickly
on guitar, so rather than trying to hold them for their full length, I place a chord voicing to emphasize
the change of chord instead.


Right-Hand Techniques for Chord Melody

Before we dive into the rest of the course, I want you to think about which plucking technique you are
going to focus on.

Because chord melody playing involves a lot of – you guessed it – chords, you need a technique that
enables you to pluck chord shapes easily.

Each plucking technique has strengths and weaknesses and gives your arrangements an overall
‘sound’ or feel.

Check out these two different versions of this chord melody excerpt of Summertime, each with a
different plucking technique.


Fingerstyle Example

In this first example, I use the fingerstyle technique.

Notice the intricate arpeggio figures I can create with this technique.

Because you can play each voice of the arrangement individually with this plucking technique, you can
get a lot of creative possibilities.

Joe Pass’ solo guitar recordings are a good example of this kind of style.

To pluck the bass notes, you can use your thumb by itself or consider Lenny Breau’s approach of
using a thumb pick. This can be helpful to play single lines easily like a plectrum player when you
need it.

Plectrum Example

You can also create an effective arrangement with plectrum only.

However, your options may be more limited as you need to find voicings where you can strum the
chord mostly as block chords rather than the more intricate polyphony of fingerstyle.

This simpler approach is not necessarily inferior, as strumming block chords in this way can give the
arrangement more drive and swing.

Herb Ellis and Wes Montgomery are classic examples of this kind of sound.



 Quick tip: When using a plectrum, pluck the bass note first, followed by a quick strum of the rest
 of the chord (you can see me doing this in the example below) – this can be a way to negotiate
 chord voicings with tricky string skips.

Hybrid Picking Example

There is a way to combine a standard plectrum with fingerstyle, called hybrid picking.

In this case, you hold a plectrum as usual but use your free fingers (the middle, ring, and pinky) to
play chord voicings whilst using the plectrum to pluck the lowest note in the chord.

Personally, I find this technique awkward especially due to the shorter length of the pinky finger.

So, for chord melody, we usually recommend using either fingerstyle or using a regular plectrum.



 Action Steps: think about which approach you would like to use for this course and what you are
 familiar with technique-wise already. Most of the examples in the course will be fingerstyle, but
 you can easily tweak the examples to use block chords suitable for the strumming plectrum style.
